variables.content: Optical absorption and photoluminescence measurements on the CsPbBr$_3$ powder indicate an optical bandgap $E_g\approx 2.26\ \text{eV}$, an exciton-related feature giving an estimated exciton binding energy $E_b\approx 170\ \text{meV}$ (difference between absorption onset and excitonic peak), and a photoluminescence Stokes shift of approximately $80\ \text{meV}$; these spectroscopic signatures imply formation of bound excitons that couple to the lattice and form polarons, and transient electrical (discharge-mode) measurements performed after a 5 V trigger pulse (discharge measured for 50 s) show that the photogenerated current scales approximately linearly with applied bias (measured at biases of 0 V, 2 V, and 5 V), a bias dependence consistent with hopping/polaron-mediated transport between localized states rather than purely band-like conduction.

Let ARPES denote angle-resolved photoemission spectroscopy measurements on layered manganites that report strong incoherent spectral weight at binding energies well below the coherent band. The proposition is that, under experimental conditions where orbital order or strong orbital fluctuations are present and electronic superexchange dominates, the incoherent ARPES features observed in certain highly doped layered manganite experiments can be attributed primarily to electronic orbital-excitation dressing (orbital polarons) rather than to small lattice (Jahn–Teller) polarons; in other words, orbital-polaron spectral signatures can mimic or dominate over lattice-polaron signatures in ARPES when orbital dynamics are the principal source of dressing. [23]

The comparison between calculated band dispersions and experimental angle-resolved photoemission spectroscopy (ARPES) data is based on experimental points available only along selected crystal directions (the paper cites measurements in the [110] direction for all compounds and additionally the [100] direction for GaAs), and the paper overlays calculated band energies with those ARPES points without performing matrix-element-weighted spectral-intensity simulations that would model how photoemission matrix elements and experimental intensity affect the observed peak positions; consequently, the mapping from the calculated band energies to measured photoemission peak positions outside the measured directions and the confidence in the absolute placement of conduction-band minima are limited by this restricted directional coverage and the implicit assumption that calculated bare band energies correspond directly to measured photoemission peak energies.
      Fig. 5 (overlay of calculated dispersions with ARPES points)

In room-temperature angle-resolved photoemission spectroscopy (ARPES) measurements on freshly cleaved single crystals of CsPbBr3, the hole effective mass at the valence-band maximum (VBM) located at the bulk Brillouin-zone R point was determined by fitting local quadratic dispersions and found to be m_{h}^{*}=0.203\pm0.016\,m_{0}, where m_{0} denotes the free-electron mass; this reported value is the average of four independent measurements on four different cleaved samples that produced individual fitted masses of 0.19\,m_{0}, 0.23\,m_{0}, 0.20\,m_{0}, and 0.19\,m_{0}. The identification of the VBM at the bulk R point was made by photon-energy-dependent ARPES mapping of the perpendicular crystal momentum k_{\perp}, and under the free-electron final-state mapping k_{\perp}=\sqrt{\tfrac{2m_{0}}{\hbar^{2}}\bigl(E_{\mathrm{kin}}\cos^{2}\theta + V_{0}\bigr)} with inner potential parameter V_{0}=5.3\ \mathrm{eV} (where \hbar is the reduced Planck constant, E_{\mathrm{kin}} the photoelectron kinetic energy referenced to the vacuum level, and \theta the electron emission polar angle), the first R point in these measurements corresponds to photon energy h\nu=29\ \mathrm{eV}.

Angle-resolved photoluminescence measured from the bulk region of a patterned CsPbBr3 perovskite valley-Hall honeycomb lattice at room temperature shows an s-mode polariton energy band extending from approximately 2.262 eV to 2.320 eV and a bandgap opening of approximately 5 meV at the two inequivalent valley points K and K' in the momentum-space spectra collected from the bulk domain.

For an orbital-ordered ferromagnetic plane at low hole doping, angle-resolved photoemission spectroscopy (ARPES) measurements should observe: (i) a coherent quasiparticle band with a bandwidth and spectral weight strongly reduced relative to the single-electron bands predicted by local density approximation (LDA) or LDA+U band-structure calculations, and (ii) pronounced incoherent spectral weight at higher binding energies whose energy structure and momentum dependence vary with the orbital ordering parameter $E_z$. Consequently, incoherent ARPES features reported in some layered manganite experiments that have been discussed in terms of small lattice (Jahn–Teller) polarons may alternatively be explained by electronic orbital-excitation dressing (orbital polarons) under the relevant experimental conditions in which orbital dynamics dominate.

The polished CsPbBr3 single crystal emits bright visible photoluminescence at room temperature when carriers are generated by three-photon absorption; the PL spectral shape produced under 3PA is essentially identical to the PL spectrum produced by one-photon excitation (showing band-edge recombination near the direct gap E_{g}=2.25 eV), and the PL spectral shape remains invariant as the fundamental excitation wavelength is tuned across the measured 3PA band, while the PL intensity exhibits pronounced increases at the two-photon and three-photon absorption band edges.

Angle-resolved photoemission spectroscopy (ARPES) measured on polished 3C-SiC(111) shows a linear π-band dispersion at the graphene Brillouin-zone K point that is characteristic of a single graphene layer; that ARPES spectrum was recorded from the ARPES instrument sampling region (the ARPES probe spot or illuminated sampling region), and this single-spot band-structure measurement is used together with LEEM mapping to assert monolayer electronic structure across larger ~50×50 μm^2 LEEM-mapped regions.
      Fig. 4(d) inset (ARPES spectrum)

The longitudinal optical (LO) phonon mode with energy $\hbar\omega=18\ \text{meV}$ is defined as the lattice vibration whose eigenvector displaces Pb and Br atoms along specific directions in the orthorhombic cell, and prior experimental and theoretical evidence (including angle-resolved photoemission spectroscopy and electron–phonon coupling calculations) identifies this 18 meV LO mode as the phonon most strongly coupled to charge carriers in CsPbBr₃; accordingly, displacing the lattice along the 18 meV LO eigenvector constitutes the physically motivated candidate to model a photoinduced polaronic structural distortion.
      Puppin et al., Phys. Rev. Lett. 2020; ARPES evidence cited in the paper

Depending on the electron–phonon coupling strength, a completely filled valence band can develop (a) a single momentum-space window centered at the valence-band top caused by u-polarons at weak coupling, and (b) two windows — one around the band top due to u-polarons and one around the band bottom due to d-polarons — at strong coupling; each window is accompanied by a corresponding polaron quasiparticle peak in angle-resolved photoemission spectroscopy (ARPES), so that the number of observed quasiparticle maxima equals the number of windows (one in the weak-coupling case, two in the strong-coupling case). A macroscopic population of autolocalized valence electrons modifies the phonon vacuum and produces a lattice deformation that can stabilize superstructures or change crystal symmetry (including generation of incommensurate structures). Upon heating above the polaron thermal-destruction temperature $T_{c}$ the valence polarons are thermally destroyed, the windows close, the quasiparticle peaks disappear, and the high-symmetry (undistorted) band structure and lattice symmetry are restored.
